summary: O'Neil reflects on NPR's 'Bored and Brilliant' challenge, which encouraged adults to reduce phone usage to foster creativity through boredom. While the challenge showed minimal impact on actual phone habits, she argues its value lies in shifting attitudes about boredom. She then pivots to question why parents who recognize boredom's creative benefits still overschedule their children, noting that she deliberately keeps her older kids unscheduled to allow for the kind of unstructured time that fueled her own childhood creativity.
key_points: The 'Bored and Brilliant' challenge had minimal measurable impact on participants' actual phone usage
Even without behavioral change, shifting cultural attitudes about boredom has value
There's a contradiction between adults seeking boredom for creativity while overscheduling their children
Children are better positioned than adults to benefit from boredom—fewer responsibilities, more creative potential
O'Neil's best childhood experiences emerged from unstructured, adult-plan-free time
money_quotes: your most creative moments happen when you're bored
I always cherish the couple of days when I've lost my phone but before I get my new one. Sweet freedom!
why don't we let our kids get bored if we think it's so great?
I deliberately keep my kids virtually unscheduled outside of their school and homework
kids aren't addicted to phones (yet), and they don't have as many family responsibilities, and they do have plenty of reasons and avenues to be incredibly creative
My best moments of childhood – playing music, forming lifelong friendships, reading Dostoyevsky, and yes, experimenting with things – only happened because I was utterly without other grown-up plans
key_insight: Adults who recognize boredom's creative value for themselves often fail to extend the same gift to their children, whose unstructured time may matter even more.
